12291.history: While in service with the Royal Navy LST 404 was torpedoed and damaged on 15 August 1944 by the German submarine U-741 about 35 nautical miles south-east of St. Catherine’s Point in position 50º02'N, 00º38'W. The damaged ship was towed to Lee-on-Solent and declared a total loss after breaking in two.

12301.history: HMS LST 414 was torpedoed and damaged at 0335 hours on 15 August 1943 by Italian aircraft off Bizerte, Tunisia. The ship was beached and abandoned. She was formally paid off on 14 November 1943. The Italian aircraft was an S.79 torpedo bomber belonging to the 278^ Sqn,132nd Group, piloted bv captain Carlo Faggioni, which had taken off from Decimomannu airfield, Sardinia.
commanders: T/A/Lt.Cdr. Cecil Albert Payne, RNR (8 Mar 1943 - Aug 1943)
Lt. Howard Baskerville Cadogan, RNR (Aug 1943 - 15 Aug 1943)

12305.history: At 1511 hours on 16 February 1944, HMS LST-418 was struck by a Gnat from the German submarine U-230 and sank off Anzio, Italy in position 41º00'N, 12º55'E after being hit by a coup de grâce at 1536 hours. The vessel was participating in the landings in Anzio-Nettuno, Operation Shingle. Hit by U-boatSunk on 16 Feb 1944 by U-230 (Siegmann).
commanders: T/A/Lt.Cdr. Cyril Charles Reynolds, RNR (Aug 1943 - 16 Feb 1944)

12307.history: Completed in June 1937. Displacement: 2376 GRT Armamant: 6 4" AA guns (3x2), 8 2pdr AA (2x4) In the early morning of 12 November, HMS Tynwald (Capt. (retired) Philip George Wodehouse, DSO, RN) was at short notice, ready to sail from 0445 hrs onwards, in the expectation of an Axis air raid at dawn. She was anchored next to monitor HMS Roberts. A scant 30' later she was hit by 2 torpedoes fired by Italian submarine Argo (Lt. Pasquale Gigli), which had penetrated the roads. Tynwald settled rapidly in 7 metres of water with 10 dead, survivors were rescued by Roberts and corvette HMS Samphire.
commanders: A/Capt. (retired) Frederic Thornton Peters, DSO, DSC, RN (1 Aug 1941 - 24 Aug 1942)
Capt. (retired) Philip George Wodehouse, DSO, RN (24 Aug 1942 - 11 Nov 1942)

12333.history: Completed in January 1938. Displacement: 1893 GRT Armamant: 8 4" AA guns (4x2), 8 2pdr AA (2x4) In August 1940 Pozarica was requisitioned by the Royal Navy and converted to Anti-Aircraft ship. In the afternoon of 29 January 1943, HMS Pozarica (Capt. (retired) Laurence Bernard Hill, DSO, OBE, RN) was escorting coastal convoy TF.14, running between Algerian ports. Off Bougie, the convoy was attacked by 2 squadrons of torpedo planes, one made up by 13 German a/c (10 He.111 and 3 Ju.88 from I and III/KG.26), the other consisting of 8 Italian S.79 from the 105th, 130th and 132nd Groups. 2 of the escorts, destroyer Avon Vale and Flakship Pozarica were hit by, respectively, the German and by the Italian planes. Avon Vale, hit @ 2110 hrs in 37.01 N, 04.44 E, was forced to run herself aground with the bows totally wrecked, while Pozarica hit at the stern @ 1945 hrs in 37.04 N, 04.36 E, managed to reach Bougie roads. On 13 February, while under salvage, Pozarica suddenly capsized and settled on the bottom. The wreck was refloated postwar and towed to Italy for scrapping, beginning in May 1951.
commanders: A/Capt. Edmund Francis Fitzgerald, RN (15 Feb 1941 - 10 Mar 1941)
A/Capt. (retired) Edward Douglas Wyndham Lawford, RN (10 Mar 1941 - 2 Oct 1942)
Capt. (retired) Laurence Bernard Hill, DSO, OBE, RN (2 Oct 1942 - 13 Feb 1943)
